title: Generic high-entropy secrets should not be disclosed
description: This rule detects high-entropy strings assigned to variable names that strongly suggest they are secrets, even when no specific service prefix is detectable. Real secrets have high randomness (entropy) unlike human-readable values. Combined with secret-suggesting variable names, these patterns reliably identify hardcoded credentials that may not match any known service format.
recommendation: Review the flagged value to confirm it is a secret. If confirmed, move it to an environment variable or secret manager. Avoid hardcoding any secret regardless of service type.
impact: Potential unauthorized access to services and systems depending on what the exposed secret controls.

checks.message: High-entropy string assigned to a secret-named variable detected. The variable name strongly suggests this is a cryptographic key, API secret, or authentication token. Random-looking strings assigned to variables with names like 'secret_key', 'api_secret', or 'signing_key' are almost certainly hardcoded credentials. Move this value to an environment variable or secrets manager and rotate it if it has been committed to version control.

Client secret, consumer secret, or webhook signing secret detected in variable assignment. These credential types are used for OAuth authentication, webhook signature verification, and JWT signing. Exposing them allows forging of OAuth requests, verification bypass for webhooks, and JWT token generation. Rotate immediately and move to secure storage.
